participants.name: AI
participants.professional_persona: AI consultant at a boutique firm, helping SMEs assess AI readiness using tools like AIRI and then prototyping solutions. Juggles multiple short projects across logistics, retail, and manufacturing.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Broad knowledge of supervised and unsupervised learning, cloud AI services, and business process mapping.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Hopes to build a reputation as a go-to AI strategist for Singapore’s SME sector and later start his own consultancy.

participants.name: Dr. (udxyqn)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Ng is a Director of AI Research at a health-tech startup, where she develops AI models for drug discovery and personalised medicine. She earned her PhD in bioinformatics from a top Asian university.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in graph neural networks, molecular modelling, and multi-omics data integration. Experienced in leading cross-disciplinary teams of biologists and data scientists.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to accelerate the discovery of novel therapeutics using AI, and to build a world-class AI-for-drug-discovery lab in Singapore.

participants.name: Raj (rxpsyj)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-rxpsyjl7
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-rxpsyjl7
participants.professional_persona: Raj is a product designer at a fintech startup, passionate about inclusive design. He believes AI can make financial services accessible for elderly and low-income Singaporeans.
participants.skills_and_expertise: He has skills in user research, wireframing, and prototyping, and recently completed an AI literacy workshop by Google Cloud's AI Trailblazers. He can articulate user needs to data scientists.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Raj wants to create an AI-powered financial literacy app targeted at seniors. He plans to apply for a co-funded project under AISG's 100 Experiments to build a prototype.

participants.name: Mr.
participants.professional_persona: Mr. Lee is a Machine Learning Engineer at a logistics SME, having completed the AI Apprenticeship Programme (AIAP) under AI Singapore. He transitioned from a software engineering role and now develops AI solutions for supply chain optimisation.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Practical skills in computer vision, object detection, and time-series forecasting. Hands-on experience with deploying models on edge devices in warehouse settings.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to deepen his research skills and eventually pursue a part-time PhD, aiming to become a subject matter expert in logistics AI.

participants.name: Dr. (dnatao)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Teo is a Research Scientist at an AI research institute focused on responsible AI and fairness. She holds a PhD in social computing and has published extensively on bias mitigation in AI systems.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expertise in algorithmic fairness, accountability, and transparency. Skilled in qualitative research methods and policy analysis.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aims to shape national and international standards for responsible AI, and to ensure that AI benefits all segments of Singapore's society equitably.

initial_posts.narrative: As a director of AI research in a health-tech startup, I see NAIS 2.0's inputs as foundational for enabling AI experimentation and talent development. The policy context states over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D, which I take as the total funding. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is explicitly stated. For compute, given the emphasis on industry use and the need for substantial GPU hours for AI model training (especially in drug discovery), I estimate 5 million GPU hours as a reasonable allocation for the programme's first year, considering typical cloud GPU cluster sizes and usage patterns. For trainers, based on the need to guide enterprises and practitioners across multiple sectors, I estimate 200 AI experts and trainers, which aligns with scaling up from existing programmes like AIAP.
evidence: The policy context explicitly states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D', so I use that as the total funding.
The policy context states 'co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', which sets the max co-funding per project.
Given the compute infrastructure investment and typical GPU cluster capacities (e.g., 100-200 GPUs running 24/7), 5 million GPU hours is a plausible annual allocation for AI experimentation.
To support a national programme with multiple tracks, 200 trainers is a reasonable estimate based on the scale of similar government-led AI upskilling initiatives.

initial_posts.narrative: As a research scientist focused on responsible AI, I view the inputs through the lens of ensuring equitable access and practical support for diverse enterprises. The policy context clearly states over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D, so I anchor total_funding_sgd at 500,000,000. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is also explicitly stated. For compute, while Dr. (udxyqn) estimates 5 million GPU hours, I believe a more conservative 3 million GPU hours is realistic for the first year, given that compute allocation must balance industry experimentation with research needs and infrastructure ramp-up. For trainers, I estimate 150 personnel, lower than 200, because scaling up from existing programmes like AIAP requires careful recruitment and training of qualified AI experts, especially those with responsible AI expertise. My perspective emphasizes that quality and diversity of trainers matter more than raw numbers for effective enterprise guidance.
evidence: The policy context explicitly states over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D, so total_funding_sgd is anchored at 500,000,000.

Compute infrastructure for AI experiments typically requires phased deployment; 3 million GPU hours is a reasonable first-year allocation given typical cloud GPU cluster sizes and the need to reserve capacity for research and responsible AI audits.
Trainer personnel count of 150 reflects the challenge of sourcing AI experts with both technical and responsible AI skills, and the need to maintain quality over quantity in enterprise guidance.

initial_posts.narrative: As a product designer focused on inclusive AI, I see the inputs as the foundation for enabling real-world AI solutions that serve diverse communities. The policy context clearly states over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D, so I anchor total_funding_sgd at 500,000,000. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is also explicitly stated. This accounts for the need to scale up from existing programmes like AIAP while ensuring quality and diversity of expertise, especially in responsible AI and inclusive design.

initial_posts.narrative: As a responsible AI researcher, I see the 100E programme's goal of generating 100 use cases in 100 days as ambitious but requiring careful, quality-focused execution. The S$500M funding and 4M GPU hours are substantial, but the 150 trainers I estimate (lower than the consensus of 180) reflect my concern about sourcing experts with responsible AI skills. Given the need to engage diverse enterprises, including those in underserved sectors, I estimate 60 outreach sessions—fewer than the 80 others suggest—because I believe deeper, more targeted engagement with fewer enterprises yields better problem statements that avoid off-the-shelf solutions. For matchmaking, 20 events suffice to pair researchers with challenges, as quality pairings require careful curation. Training workshops at 40 reflect a focus on hands-on, responsible AI content rather than volume. Platform onboarding assists at 150 are sufficient to support the enterprises and researchers actively using the platform, given the compute infrastructure. My estimates are lower than Dr. (udxyqn), Mr. (AIResearcher), and Raj (rxpsyj) because I prioritize depth over breadth to ensure equitable and responsible outcomes.
evidence: The 100E programme requires problem statements with no off-the-shelf AI solution, so targeted outreach to 60 enterprises is more effective than broad sessions to find genuinely novel challenges.

With 150 trainers (my estimate from prior phase), conducting 40 workshops ensures each workshop has adequate trainer-to-participant ratio for quality hands-on learning, especially on responsible AI topics.
Matchmaking 20 events is sufficient to pair researchers with enterprise challenges, as each event can facilitate multiple pairings and careful matching reduces mismatches.
Platform onboarding assists at 150 align with the active user base from 60 outreach sessions and 20 matchmaking events, providing tailored support without overstretching resources.

initial_posts.narrative: As an AI consultant working hands-on with SMEs, I see the 100E programme's 100-use-cases-in-100-days target as aggressive but achievable with focused, quality-driven outreach. The S$500M funding, 4M GPU hours, and 180 trainers provide strong resources, but my experience with SMEs shows that many need substantial hand-holding to articulate problem statements that avoid off-the-shelf solutions. I estimate 70 enterprise outreach sessions—fewer than the 80 others suggest—because I believe deeper, more targeted engagement with fewer companies yields better problem statements and avoids wasting resources on superficial participation. For matchmaking, 25 events strike a balance between pairing researchers with challenges and ensuring quality curation, given the need for careful alignment of expertise with enterprise needs. Training workshops at 45 reflect scaling up from existing programmes like AIAP, but I lean slightly lower than the 50 others propose because I prioritize hands-on, practical content over volume, especially for SMEs that may lack technical depth. Platform onboarding assists at 180 are sufficient to support active users, given that not all enterprises will immediately adopt the platform; my experience with AIRI assessments shows that many SMEs need gradual onboarding. Dr. (dnatao)'s lower estimates resonate with my focus on depth, but I find 60 outreach sessions too conservative for the 100E target—70 is a pragmatic middle ground. The consensus of 80 from Dr. (udxyqn), Mr. (AIResearcher), and Raj (rxpsyj) seems optimistic given the limited pool of SMEs ready for AI experimentation and the need for quality over quantity.
evidence: My work with SMEs using the AI Readiness Index shows that many need multiple touchpoints to identify viable AI problem statements, so 70 outreach sessions allow for deeper engagement with fewer companies, avoiding superficial participation.

The 100E programme's co-funding cap of S$330K per project and the need for careful researcher-enterprise alignment justify 25 matchmaking events, as quality pairings require curation rather than volume.

Training workshops at 45 reflect scaling from existing programmes like AIAP, but I prioritize hands-on, practical content for SMEs, which limits the number of workshops that can be delivered effectively with 180 trainers.

Platform onboarding assists at 180 are realistic because not all enterprises will immediately adopt the platform; my experience shows that gradual, personalized support is more effective than mass onboarding.

revised_posts.narrative: As a health-tech AI research director, I see NAIS 2.0's impact as tangible but tempered by real-world constraints. The strong inputs (S$500M, 5M GPU hours, 200 trainers) and activities (80 outreach, 30 matchmaking, 50 workshops, 200 onboarding) led to 60 approved 100E projects, 45 prototypes, 150 trainees, and 40 enterprises with solutions. From these, 30 projects reached production deployment, the AI practitioner pool reached 13,000, 85 generative AI use cases were created, and enterprise adoption hit 20%. These outcomes, while below aspirational targets, signal clear momentum. Singapore's global AI readiness rank improves to 5th, reflecting its proactive strategy and infrastructure investments, though deployment lags in regulated sectors like health-tech. The AI sector's GDP share reaches 3.5%, driven by deployed solutions and growing adoption, especially in logistics and finance. Public trust in AI systems is at 65%, supported by responsible AI training and governance, but concerns about job displacement and data privacy persist. AI talent retention is 70%, as the AIAP and other programmes create attractive opportunities, but some talent is drawn to global tech hubs. My estimates align with Mr. (AIResearcher), AI (AIPractitioner), and Raj (rxpsyj) because the strong resource base and practical deployments are yielding visible benefits. I disagree with Dr. (dnatao)'s more conservative estimates (rank 6, 3.0%, 60%, 65%) because my health-tech experience shows that even with regulatory hurdles, the compute and trainer resources enable enough successful deployments to boost economic impact and trust. The 30 deployed projects and 85 generative AI use cases provide concrete examples that improve Singapore's standing and public confidence.
evidence: With 30 deployed 100E projects and 85 generative AI use cases, Singapore demonstrates practical AI leadership, improving its global readiness rank to 5th, as seen in similar national AI strategies.

The AI sector's GDP share of 3.5% is driven by the 40 enterprises with AI solutions and 20% enterprise adoption rate, consistent with the economic impact of deployed AI in sectors like logistics and health-tech.

Public trust at 65% is supported by responsible AI training in the 50 workshops and 200 onboarding assists, though concerns about job displacement and data privacy limit higher trust, as seen in my health-tech deployments.
AI talent retention of 70% is achieved through the AIAP's 150 completers and attractive career pathways, but some talent leaves for global tech hubs, reflecting the competitive international market.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group discussed that NAIS 2.0's substantial investments in compute, training, and co-funding, combined with the 100E programme's practical deployments, are driving tangible outcomes such as 25-30 deployed projects, 80-85 generative AI use cases, and 18-20% enterprise adoption. These outcomes are improving Singapore's global AI readiness rank and increasing the AI sector's GDP share, while responsible AI training and governance are building public trust, though concerns about job displacement and data privacy persist. AI talent retention is supported by attractive career pathways but limited by competition from global tech hubs. There was consensus on a global AI readiness rank of 5, AI sector GDP share of 3.5%, public trust of 65%, and talent retention of 70%, with one dissenting voice estimating more conservative values of rank 6, 3.0%, 60%, and 65% due to slower deployment from responsible AI audits and integration challenges. The central tendency across predictions was rank 5, GDP share 3.5%, trust 65%, and retention 70%, with a range of rank 5-6, GDP share 3.0-3.5%, trust 60-65%, and retention 65-70%.
